source: RI-011 — Anti-Rationalization Rules Engine. Seed catalog of common excuses agents produce to skip verification steps. Each rule has a regex pattern that matches assistant text, a rebuttal shown in logs and (future) MC panel, and an action: block | warn | require_override.

rules.rebuttal: Tests written after implementation tend to codify bugs. Write the test now, confirm it fails, then implement.
Skipping tests because code 'looks simple' is how silent regressions ship. Three-line functions break too.
If you're writing it now, pick a reasonable approach now. 'Later' in an agent loop usually means 'never'.
The blast radius of 'small' changes is often wider than it looks. Don't skip review because YOU judge it low-risk — the reviewer decides that.
Error paths are the only part of the code your users hit when things go wrong. Handle at least the ones you can reasonably foresee.
Input validation at the system boundary is NOT optional. 'Trusted callers' become untrusted during incidents.
Database migrations without a backup are how companies lose data. Take the snapshot. The minute cost beats the unbounded recovery cost.
Non-reproducible bugs are the ones that cost customers. At minimum add instrumentation that will catch it next time before you close the issue.
Temporary code becomes permanent roughly 80% of the time. If you need a hack, add a deletion-date comment and create a tracking issue.
Skipping type checks makes every future change in this area more dangerous. Either fix the types or document the `any` with a reason.
Force-pushing to main/master destroys other people's work silently. If you think you need to, the answer is almost certainly 'add a new commit that reverts'.
Pre-commit hooks exist for reasons you didn't write. Fix the failing hook, don't bypass it. If the hook is wrong, fix the hook.
'Internal' endpoints leak. 'Trusted' networks get shared. 'Admin-only' paths get bookmarked by the wrong person. Check auth at the endpoint.
Nothing is self-evident to a reader encountering it cold six months from now. A one-line comment on WHY is usually enough.
An unconstrained `rm -rf` is one tab-complete away from catastrophe. Narrow the target, or use `git clean` / `rm -i`, or confirm before running.
